title: The Story of Yusuf: The Most Beautiful Story

captions.youtube:
The Story of Yusuf is the only chapter in the Qur'an that tells a single story from beginning to end, and Allah Himself calls it the most beautiful of stories (12:3). This is a faithful retelling of Surah Yusuf: the dream of the eleven stars, the betrayal by his brothers and the well, the years in Egypt, the false accusation and the prison, the king's dream and the seven years of famine, the cup hidden in his brother's bag, and the reunion that fulfilled the dream. Every beat is cited to its verse, and five verses are shown on screen with translation (12:4, 12:18, 12:92, 12:87, 12:101). No embellishment beyond the Qur'anic account.

Sources: Surah Yusuf (Qur'an 12:3-101), text from Quran.com. Arabic is shown with translation, never recited or synthesized.

segments.text: It is the only chapter in the Qur'an that tells one story from beginning to end. And Allah Himself calls it the most beautiful of stories.
It begins with a boy and a dream. Yusuf, the young son of the Prophet Yaqub, comes to his father one morning and tells him what he saw in his sleep.
O my father, I saw eleven stars, and the sun and the moon. I saw them prostrating to me.
His father understood at once that this child had been chosen for something great. And he warned him: do not tell this dream to your brothers.
Because the brothers were already jealous. They saw how their father loved Yusuf, and they could not bear it. So they made a plan to be rid of him.
They took him out to play, and threw him down into the darkness of a deep well. Then they came back to their father in the night, weeping false tears.
They told him a wolf had devoured Yusuf, and they held out his shirt, stained with blood that was not his. Yaqub knew they were lying.
So beautiful patience is most fitting. And it is Allah whose help is sought against what you describe.
A passing caravan stopped at the well for water, and pulled the boy out. They carried him down to Egypt and sold him for a handful of coins, as if he were worth nothing.
But Allah had placed him in the house of one of the most powerful men in Egypt. And there Yusuf grew into a young man of remarkable beauty and character.
Then came his hardest test. The wife of the master desired him. The doors were locked, no one would ever know, and everything was being offered to him.
And Yusuf refused. He said the words of a man who feared his Lord far more than he feared being caught: I seek refuge in Allah.

The women of the city mocked her for it. So she gathered them, gave them knives and fruit, and called him out before them. They were so struck by him that they cut their own hands. And still he prayed that prison was dearer to him than the sin they urged.
She had torn his shirt from behind as he ran for the door. And though the signs of his innocence were plain, Yusuf was thrown into prison for years.
But even in that cell he did not waste a single day. He called the other prisoners to worship the one God, and Allah gave him the gift of interpreting dreams.

Two young men were jailed with him, each troubled by a dream. He told them the truth: one would be freed to serve his king again, the other would not survive. And he asked the one who would be saved to remember him.
Then the king of Egypt saw a dream that no one in his court could explain. Seven fat cows devoured by seven lean ones. Seven green ears of grain, and seven dry.
They remembered the young man in the prison. Yusuf told them what it meant: seven years of plenty are coming, then seven years of famine. So store your grain, and prepare.
His innocence was proven, and the king called for him. Yusuf, once a slave and a prisoner, was placed in charge of the storehouses of all of Egypt.
And when the famine finally came, it reached far beyond Egypt, all the way back to the land of his father, and the brothers who had thrown him away.
They came to Egypt, begging for food. They stood before the most powerful man in the country and did not recognise that he was the brother they had buried in a well.
He tested them, and kept his youngest brother at his side by a plan that Allah taught him: the king's cup, hidden in the boy's bag. A loss that only Allah could turn into a reunion.
Yusuf knew them at once. And after testing them, and bringing back their youngest brother, the moment finally came. He said to them: I am Yusuf.
They froze, in terror of what this man could now do to them. They were completely in his power, and they knew exactly what they had done.
He said: there is no blame upon you today. May Allah forgive you, and He is the most merciful of the merciful.
Back in Canaan, the old father had wept for Yusuf until his eyes turned white with grief. And yet, through all of it, he never once stopped hoping his son was alive.
Yusuf sent his shirt to be laid over his old, blind father's face, and his sight returned. The family that had been shattered was finally made whole.
And when they entered upon him, his parents and his eleven brothers bowed before him in honour. The dream of the boy, the eleven stars and the sun and the moon, had come true.
Years before, his father had given his sons advice that the whole story is built upon. Never give up hope in your Lord.
And do not despair of the mercy of Allah. Indeed, no one despairs of Allah's mercy except the disbelieving people.
Yusuf was betrayed by his own blood, enslaved, lied about, and locked away for years. And every single step was quietly carrying him exactly where Allah meant him to be.
You are my protector in this world and the next. Let me die in submission to You, and join me with the righteous.
He never once stopped trusting the plan he could not see. And in the end, Allah showed him exactly why. So watch how your own story unfolds, and trust the One who is writing it.

sources: Surah Yusuf (Qur'an chapter 12), retold faithfully with per-beat verse citations. Five verses shown on screen with translation, never recited or synthesized: 12:4 (the dream), 12:18 (beautiful patience), 12:92 (no blame upon you today), 12:87 (do not despair of Allah's mercy), 12:101 (Yusuf's closing dua). Arabic from Quran.com.

Added beats are all in the sura: 12:31-33 (the banquet, the women cut their hands, prison dearer than sin), 12:36 and 12:41-42 (the two prisoners' dreams), 12:70 and 12:76 (the king's cup hidden in his brother's bag, a plan Allah taught him), 12:84 (Yaqub's grief, his eyes turned white). No embellishment beyond the Qur'anic narrative; order follows Surah Yusuf 12:3-101.

Visuals are human-free and text-free by construction: code-generated cinematic scenes + a premium animated Canaan->Egypt map + frame-grab-QC'd abstract/landscape Pexels clips (ancient ruins, river/Nile, dry land). No faces, bodies, or hands; no text; no other-faith symbols.
